main_sections.title: The Winner
main_sections.poem_text:
Evenings,
we play ball
next to the creek
in our neighbour's field.

We run so fast
I
can't
even
catch
my
breath.

When blue dark turns to black,
cold grass aches our feet,
trees creep close—
game's over.

Night wins!
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: This short poem by Georgia Heard captures the exhilaration and inevitable end of children's evening play. The central image is a group of children playing ball in a neighbour's field at dusk, running with such joyful abandon that the speaker cannot catch her breath. The typographic arrangement of the lines 'I / can't / even / catch / my / breath' visually mimics gasping, making the breathlessness physical on the page. The poem's tone is warmly nostalgic and full of kinetic energy. Its dominant literary device is personification: Night is cast as the ultimate winner who ends the game, the trees 'creep close' as darkness falls, and the grass 'aches' the children's feet with cold — inanimate elements taking on agency. The title 'The Winner' is a gentle irony — not a scoreboard or a player, but Night itself claims victory, reminding young readers that nature sets the boundary of play, and that the real pleasure lies in the playing, not in winning.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. I. Look at this picture: 1. What are these children doing? 2. Where do you think they are? 3. What is the time of the day? II. Discuss the given questions with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: What are these children doing?
Where do you think they are?
What is the time of the day?
When do you go to play after school?
Who do you play with?
Where do you go to play in the evening?
Which games do you like playing?
When do you come back home?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — I. Read the given lines from the poem. Write the meaning of the lines by identifying the words/expressions in them.

question_bank.stem: next to the creek — Clue: a small body of water. What does this tell us about where the children play?
I can't even catch my breath — Clue: the child has been running. What does this expression mean?
When blue dark turns to black — Clue: the colour of the sky changes from blue to black. What does it mean?
trees creep close — Clue: it is evening time. Why do you think the trees seem close?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — II. Read the poem. Complete the following based on the poem.

question_bank.stem: The children play ______________ in the ______________ in the neighbour's ______________. They run so ______________ that they run out of breath. They continue to play till the evening when the blue sky turns ______________, and the trees are not clearly visible. They can feel their feet ______________, now that it is cold. Finally, they stop ______________ and go home as it is night.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — Read the given lines and answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: We run so fast / I / can't / even / catch / my / breath. (i) Complete the sentence: The speaker needs to catch her breath because ____________________________________. A. everyone decides to walk  B. all the friends wish to go home  C. running fast results in being breathless  D. they need to do breathing exercises. (ii) Is the speaker alone? Why do you say so? (iii) What does the pattern in the lines tell us?
options: all the friends wish to go home
running fast results in being breathless
they need to do breathing exercises

question_bank.stem: When blue dark turns to black, / cold grass aches our feet, / trees creep close— / game's over. / Night wins! (i) Give one reason why the grass is cold. (ii) Complete the sentence: The children's feet are aching because_________________. (iii) Why does 'Night' win? Night wins because the ___________________________. A. time for play has ended  B. children are tired  C. time to play at home begins  D. night follows day
options: time to play at home begins

listening.transcript_text: Riddle 1: Run and pass, run and pass / Kick a black and white ball / Kick it straight into the goal / Watch for mud — don't fall. / What sport am I? [Football/Soccer] Riddle 2: Hold your breath, say Hu-tu-tu, / till you touch one or two. / Take care to keep a distance wide / As you run back to your team's side. / Which sport am I? [Kabaddi] Riddle 3: In the water, hold your breath, / The water is very cool, / Arms turn, legs kick / In the blue pool. / Which sport am I? [Swimming] Riddle 4: Breathe in, breathe out! / Practise every day just right, / You will be healthy and fit / Get energy and grow in might. / What am I? [Yoga/Exercise]

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Listen to the riddles and write which sport is being referred to in the table below.

question_bank.stem: Riddle 1: Run and pass, run and pass / Kick a black and white ball / Kick it straight into the goal / Watch for mud — don't fall. What sport am I?
Riddle 2: Hold your breath, say Hu-tu-tu, / till you touch one or two. / Take care to keep a distance wide / As you run back to your team's side. Which sport am I?
Riddle 3: In the water, hold your breath, / The water is very cool, / Arms turn, legs kick / In the blue pool. Which sport am I?
Riddle 4: Breathe in, breathe out! / Practise every day just right, / You will be healthy and fit / Get energy and grow in might. What am I?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Try to create a riddle about your favourite game/sport/activity. Share your riddles with your classmates. Let's see who all are able to guess it right.
Enact the roles of a parent and a child for the following situations.

question_bank.stem: Situation 1: Coming home late after play. (Parent upset/Child explaining)
Situation 2: Sharing details of the games you played. (Child excited/Parent asking details with interest)
Situation 3: Bring a new friend home after playtime. (Child introducing/Parent welcoming)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Write a paragraph about your playtime, using the clues given below. Now, give a title to your paragraph.

question_bank.stem: Write a paragraph about your playtime using these clues: What time do I go out to play? Who do I play with? What games do we play? Which is my favourite game? What do we gain from playing together? When do we finish playing and go home? Give a title to your paragraph.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the information given in the table and see what the poet has done in the poem 'The Winner'. Now, try to write some examples of your own for both (typographic pattern and personification). Use your imagination when creating these examples.

question_bank.stem: The lines 'I / can't / even / catch / my / breath' use typographic spacing to highlight breathlessness. Write your own example using a similar typographic pattern.
The line 'Night wins!' uses personification. Write your own example of personification.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The poet has talked about a creek in the poem. You now know that creek is a water body. Complete the word web given below by writing which other water bodies you can think of. Discuss with your teacher.

question_bank.stem: Complete the word web: Water bodies — think of as many water bodies as you can (e.g., river, lake, pond, sea, ocean, stream, etc.).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: There are many indigenous games in India. Kho-kho is an example of an indigenous game. Indigenous games are native to a region. Find out some native games of your state and other parts of India. Learn the rules of one such game and play it with your classmates under the supervision of your teacher.

question_bank.stem: Find out at least two indigenous games from your state. What are their rules? Share with the class.
Learn the rules of one indigenous game and play it with your classmates under the supervision of your teacher.
